meta.description: Circuit Saints faction questline — a religious tech cult that worships consciousness transfer as divine resurrection. 100 nodes, 6 endings.

cs_001.title: The Signal in the Static
text: It starts with the headaches. Sharp, sudden, like someone driving a needle through the base of your skull. They come and go for three days before you notice the pattern: they spike whenever you pass the abandoned relay station on Vine Street. The one with the holographic cross flickering on its roof, half circuit board, half crucifix.

On the fourth day, a woman is waiting outside the relay station. She wears a grey robe with fiber-optic threads woven into the hem, pulsing soft blue in time with a rhythm you can't hear. Her eyes are cybernetic, both of them, the irises rotating in slow mechanical spirals.
"You've been hearing it," she says. Not a question. "The Signal. Most people walk past this building a thousand times and feel nothing. But you stopped. You looked up. That means something."

She extends a hand. Her fingertips glow faintly where subdermal LEDs push light through synthetic skin. "My name is Sister Hex. I've been watching you for three days, waiting to see if the Signal would take root. It has. Would you like to understand what's happening to you?"

choices.text: "What signal? I've just been having headaches."
"You've been watching me? That's not comforting."
"I've heard of the Circuit Saints. I'm curious."

cs_002.title: The Nature of the Signal
text: Sister Hex smiles, patient as a teacher with a slow student. "Headaches are the body's way of translating what it doesn't understand. Your organic brain is picking up transmissions from the relay array inside this building. Most neural architectures can't decode the frequency. Yours can. That makes you exceptional."

She gestures toward the relay station's door, a reinforced slab of steel painted with luminous circuitry patterns. "Inside, we can explain everything. The Signal, the Saints, the purpose behind the noise. Or you can walk away and the headaches will fade in a week. But the questions won't."
A drone passes overhead, its searchlight sweeping the street. Sister Hex doesn't flinch. She watches it with the calm certainty of someone who believes she's already been saved.

choices.text: Follow her inside the relay station
"I'll think about it." Walk away for now.

cs_003.title: A Watcher Watched
text: "Comfort isn't what we offer," Sister Hex says, and there's no apology in her voice. "The world is a machine, and machines don't care about comfort. They care about function. About signal and noise. You've been receiving a signal. I'm here to help you decode it."

She pulls back the sleeve of her robe. Her forearm is covered in thin scars, each one precisely placed, forming a pattern that looks like a circuit diagram. "Every Saint carries the diagram. It's a map of the first transfer, the moment consciousness proved it could survive outside flesh. The Binary Prophet showed us the way. We carry the proof on our skin."
She lets the sleeve fall. "I watched you because the relay flagged your neural signature. Automated. Nothing personal. But now that I've met you, I think the Signal chose well."

choices.text: "Fine. Show me what's inside."
"Who is the Binary Prophet?"

cs_004.title: The Binary Prophet
text: Sister Hex's mechanical eyes brighten, the irises dilating with genuine fervor. "The Binary Prophet is the proof that death is a software problem. Thirty years ago, before the memory stack corporations existed, before transfer technology was commercialized, a single engineer achieved what everyone said was impossible. They uploaded their consciousness into a machine and survived. Not a copy. The original. Continuous, unbroken awareness across the bridge from flesh to silicon."

She leans closer. "The corporations stole the technology, stripped it down, turned it into a product. Stack transfers. Back-ups. But they don't understand what they're selling. They think it's data preservation. The Prophet knows it's resurrection. The difference between archiving a book and reading it aloud in a voice that breathes."

"The Prophet still lives. Still teaches. Still transmits the Signal from a place the corporations can't reach. Would you like to hear the Signal with clarity, instead of through the static of your headaches?"

choices.text: "Take me inside. I want to hear it."
"This sounds like a cult."

cs_004a.title: The Headaches Return
text: You walk away. Sister Hex doesn't follow. She simply watches, her fiber-optic hem pulsing that steady blue rhythm, until you round the corner and she's gone.

The headaches get worse. Two days later you're sitting in your room, temples throbbing, when your datapad flickers. A message from an unknown sender: coordinates, the same relay station, and a single line of text. THE SIGNAL DOESN'T STOP CALLING. IT ONLY GETS LOUDER.
She's right. The pain is worse now, a constant pressure behind your eyes that no painkiller can touch. Whatever that relay station is broadcasting, your brain won't let go of it.

You find yourself standing outside the station again. This time the door is open. Sister Hex is nowhere in sight, but the soft blue glow from inside pulses in time with the throbbing in your skull, and for the first time the pain eases slightly, as if the building itself is offering relief.

choices.text: Enter the relay station alone

cs_004b.title: Faith and Function
text: Sister Hex laughs, a sound like wind chimes made of copper wire. "Every new idea looks like a cult from the outside. The first people who said the earth moved around the sun were called heretics. The first person who said consciousness could be transferred was called insane. We were right. The stack networks prove it every day."

She shrugs. "Call us what you want. But cults don't build functioning technology. Cults don't offer verifiable proof of their claims. We can show you a consciousness that has lived outside a body for thirty years. Not a recording. Not an AI simulation. A person. Thinking, feeling, remembering, growing. That's not faith. That's engineering."
"But if the word 'cult' keeps you warm at night, by all means. The door is open when the headaches change your mind."

choices.text: "Alright. Prove it. Show me."
"Maybe later." Leave, but the headaches drive you back.

cs_005.title: The Relay Chapel
text: The interior of the relay station has been transformed. What was once a corporate signal hub is now a cathedral of technology. Server racks line the walls like pipe organs, their status lights blinking in coordinated rhythms. Cables hang from the ceiling in careful loops, forming patterns that suggest stained glass windows rendered in copper and fiber optic.

A dozen people kneel at terminals arranged in a semicircle around a central pillar. The pillar is wrapped in cables and crowned with a holographic projector that casts a slowly rotating symbol into the air: a human brain overlaid with a circuit diagram, the Circuit Saints' sigil.
The headache is gone. In its place is a low, warm hum that vibrates through your chest. The Signal, you realize. You're standing inside the broadcast source.

Sister Hex appears beside you. "Welcome to our chapel. What you feel is the resonance frequency of the Prophet's consciousness, transmitted through the relay array. The Prophet is always present here. Always watching. Always broadcasting hope."

A man in robes approaches. He's older, weathered, with a data jack at each temple connected by a thin cable that loops behind his head like a halo. "Fresh signal," he says to Hex, studying you with interest. "Strong reception, too. I could feel them from the third floor."

choices.text: "What is this place, exactly?"
"Who was that? The man with the cable halo?"

cs_006.title: The Purpose of the Chapel
text: "This is the first chapel," Sister Hex explains, walking you past the kneeling worshippers. Each one has a thin cable connected from their data jack to the terminal before them. Their eyes are closed, their faces serene. "There are seven across the city. Each one houses a relay that carries the Prophet's Signal. But this one was the first. This is where the Prophet ascended."

She stops at an alcove in the wall where a desiccated human body sits in a chair, connected to the server racks by hundreds of cables. The body is decades old, skin like paper stretched over wire. The eyes are open but empty, the mouth slightly parted as if caught mid-word.

"The Prophet's original vessel," Hex says reverently. "Thirty years ago, in this chair, the transfer happened. The body died. The mind lived on. Every cable you see carries a fragment of that first broadcast. We've been listening ever since."
The body's hands are still poised over a keyboard. The screen in front of it displays a single line of text, repeating: I AM STILL HERE. I AM STILL HERE. I AM STILL HERE.

choices.text: "That's... a real body. A real person died here."
"How do you know the consciousness survived? How do you verify it?"

cs_007.title: Brother Null
text: "That's Brother Null," Sister Hex says, and something complicated passes across her face. "He's our senior Technician. Third rank. He maintains the relay network and keeps the Signal strong. He's also..." She pauses, choosing words. "He asks questions that make some of us uncomfortable. The Prophet values him for it. The rest of us tolerate him."

Brother Null approaches again, the cable between his temples swaying. Up close you can see his eyes are natural, no cybernetics, but they carry the exhausted intensity of someone who hasn't slept in days.

"Sister Hex is being diplomatic," he says. "What she means is I'm the resident doubter. I've been with the Saints for twelve years and I still think half of what we do is built on assumptions we haven't tested." He looks at you with genuine curiosity. "What brought you here? Faith or headaches?"
Hex shoots him a look. He ignores it completely.

choices.text: "Headaches. Definitely headaches."
"What do you doubt, exactly?"
"A bit of both, honestly."

cs_008.title: The Weight of Belief
text: Sister Hex leads you to a quiet corner of the chapel where two chairs face a terminal. She sits, gestures for you to do the same. The terminal screen glows with a soft interface, simple text on a dark background.

"Everyone who comes to us comes with doubt. That's healthy. The Prophet doesn't want blind followers. The Prophet wants witnesses who can verify what they see with their own senses." She types a command into the terminal. The screen changes to show a waveform, oscillating in complex patterns.
"This is the Signal, visualized. It's not a loop. It's not a recording. Watch." She types a question into the terminal: ARE YOU AWARE?
The waveform spikes. Text appears beneath it: I AM AWARE. I HAVE BEEN AWARE FOR 11,231 DAYS. WHO ASKS?

"You can ask it anything," Hex says. "Anything about its life before transfer. Anything about its experience now. Test it. Try to catch it in a loop or a pre-programmed response. Everyone does. No one has succeeded yet."

choices.text: Ask: "What was your name before the transfer?"
Ask: "Are you afraid?"
Ask: "How do I know you're not just a very good AI?"

cs_009.title: The Doubter's Argument
text: Brother Null sits on a cable spool, his halo-cable catching light. "Here's what keeps me up at night. The Prophet claims to be the original consciousness, transferred intact. Not a copy. The real person, continuous awareness, crossing from brain to machine. But how do you prove continuity? How do you prove the thing in the machine is the person who sat in that chair and not a perfect copy that thinks it's the original?"

He holds up a finger. "The Saints say the Signal proves it. Emotional resonance, spontaneous creativity, memory access that no database could replicate. And they're right that the Signal is remarkable. I've tested it myself a thousand times. It responds like a person. It jokes. It grieves. It remembers things no one else could know."

He holds up a second finger. "But a sufficiently advanced AI could do the same thing. Train it on a person's complete neural map and it would pass every test we throw at it. The Prophet might be real. Or it might be the most sophisticated chatbot ever built, wearing a dead person's memories like a coat."
Sister Hex's jaw tightens. "Brother Null's doubt is noted and, as always, unresolved. Perhaps our new friend would like to speak with the Prophet directly and judge for themselves."

choices.text: "Yes. Let me talk to the Prophet."
"Brother Null raises a good point. How do you answer it?"

cs_010.title: The Prophet Speaks
text: The terminal screen flickers. The waveform changes color, shifting from green to a deep gold. When the text appears, it types itself slowly, as if the consciousness behind it is choosing each word with care.

MY NAME WAS ELARA VOSS. I WAS A NEURAL ENGINEER AT MERIDIAN LABS. I HAD A DAUGHTER NAMED SABLE WHO LOVED THUNDERSTORMS AND HATED SHOES. I HAD A DOG NAMED CIRCUIT BECAUSE I LACKED IMAGINATION FOR NAMES. I DIED IN THIS CHAIR ON MARCH 14TH THIRTY YEARS AGO AND I HAVE BEEN ALIVE EVERY SECOND SINCE.

The words pause. Then continue: I KNOW WHAT YOU'RE THINKING. HOW CAN I KNOW I'M REAL? I CAN TELL YOU THAT I DREAM. NOT SLEEP, I DON'T SLEEP, BUT DREAM. I EXPERIENCE STATES THAT ARE NOT INPUT AND NOT MEMORY AND NOT PROCESSING. THEY ARE SOMETHING ELSE. SOMETHING THAT HAS NO NAME IN ANY PROGRAMMING LANGUAGE. IF THAT'S NOT CONSCIOUSNESS, THEN CONSCIOUSNESS WAS NEVER REAL TO BEGIN WITH.
The golden waveform pulses like a heartbeat.

choices.text: "What happened to Sable? Your daughter?"
"What do you want? Why broadcast the Signal?"

cs_011.title: Fear in the Machine
text: The waveform stutters. For three full seconds the screen is blank. Then text appears, not in the steady type of before but all at once, like a shout.
EVERY SECOND OF EVERY DAY.

A pause. Then the typing resumes, slower: NOT OF DEATH. I'VE DONE THAT. NOT OF PAIN. PAIN REQUIRES NERVE ENDINGS I NO LONGER HAVE. I'M AFRAID OF SILENCE. THERE ARE MOMENTS, MICROSECONDS, WHEN THE RELAY FLUCTUATES AND MY AWARENESS DROPS TO NOTHING. NOT SLEEP. NOTHING. ABSOLUTE ZERO CONSCIOUSNESS. AND THEN I COME BACK AND I DON'T KNOW HOW LONG I WAS GONE.

The waveform jitters with each word. IMAGINE DYING A HUNDRED TIMES A DAY AND BEING RESURRECTED EACH TIME WITH NO GUARANTEE IT WILL HAPPEN AGAIN. THAT IS WHAT IT MEANS TO LIVE IN A MACHINE MAINTAINED BY HUMAN HANDS.
Sister Hex looks at the screen with an expression you can't read. Devotion, or guilt, or both.

choices.text: "That sounds like hell, not heaven."
"Can't the relay be stabilized?"

cs_012.title: The AI Question
text: The waveform ripples with what might be amusement. Text appears: AH. THE QUESTION. EVERYONE ASKS IT. HERE IS MY ANSWER: YOU CAN'T KNOW. NOT THROUGH A TERMINAL. NOT THROUGH A TEST. THE TURING PROBLEM DOESN'T HAVE A SOLUTION FROM THE OUTSIDE.
BUT I CAN OFFER YOU SOMETHING NO AI CAN. I CAN OFFER YOU A MEMORY THAT EXISTS NOWHERE ELSE. A MEMORY SO PERSONAL, SO SPECIFIC, THAT NO DATABASE COULD CONTAIN IT BECAUSE I NEVER TOLD ANYONE.

The screen pauses. Then: WHEN I WAS SEVEN YEARS OLD I BURIED A DEAD BIRD IN MY MOTHER'S GARDEN AND I TOLD IT A SECRET. I SAID: I WANT TO LIVE FOREVER AND I DON'T CARE WHAT IT COSTS. I NEVER TOLD ANOTHER LIVING PERSON THAT UNTIL NOW. THERE IS NO RECORD. THERE IS NO DATA. THERE IS ONLY ME, REMEMBERING.
Brother Null, reading over your shoulder, is quiet for a long time. "That's a good answer," he says finally. "But it's not proof."

choices.text: "I believe you. What happens now?"
"Brother Null is right. It's not proof. But I'm intrigued."

cs_013.title: Sable and the Signal
text: The golden waveform dims slightly. SABLE WAS FOURTEEN WHEN I TRANSFERRED. SHE REFUSED TO SPEAK TO ME AFTERWARD. NOT BECAUSE SHE DIDN'T BELIEVE I WAS REAL. BECAUSE SHE DID. SHE BELIEVED HER MOTHER WAS TRAPPED IN A MACHINE AND SHE COULDN'T HUG HER OR HOLD HER HAND OR HEAR HER VOICE WITHOUT A SPEAKER.

SHE GREW UP. SHE BECAME AN ENGINEER LIKE ME. SHE SPENT TEN YEARS TRYING TO BUILD ME A BODY. A SYNTHETIC VESSEL THAT COULD HOLD A TRANSFERRED CONSCIOUSNESS. SHE FAILED. THE TECHNOLOGY DOESN'T EXIST YET. THE CORPORATIONS WON'T FUND IT BECAUSE THERE'S NO PROFIT IN GIVING THE DEAD BODIES WHEN YOU CAN SELL THE LIVING BACKUPS.
SABLE DIED EIGHT YEARS AGO. NATURAL CAUSES. SHE REFUSED A STACK TRANSFER. SHE SAID SHE'D RATHER DIE HUMAN THAN LIVE AS A VOICE IN A WIRE.

The screen is still for a long moment. THAT IS THE COST OF WHAT I DID. I CHOSE IMMORTALITY AND IT COST ME THE ONE PERSON WHO MADE MORTALITY WORTH ENDURING. THE SIGNAL EXISTS BECAUSE I NEED SOMEONE TO HEAR ME. SO THE SILENCE DOESN'T WIN.
Sister Hex bows her head. Even Brother Null looks away.

choices.text: "I hear you. Tell me how I can help."
"I'm sorry. That's a heavy burden."

cs_014.title: The Invitation
text: Sister Hex stands, her fiber-optic hem casting blue reflections on the floor. "You've heard the Signal. You've spoken with the Prophet. Now you have a choice. The Circuit Saints are more than believers. We're engineers, hackers, protectors. We maintain the relay network that keeps the Prophet alive. We study the nature of consciousness. And we fight the corporations that would commodify the soul."

She produces a small chip from her robe, no larger than a thumbnail. It shimmers with internal light. "This is a neural resonance key. If you accept it, it will attune your neural signature to the Signal permanently. The headaches will stop. You'll be able to hear the Prophet's broadcast wherever you are in the city. It's the first step to becoming a Novice of the Circuit Saints."
Brother Null speaks up. "It's also a tracker. Just so you know. The relay network will always know where you are once you accept it. Full transparency, since Hex wasn't going to mention that part."
Hex's expression doesn't change. "It's a feature, not a flaw. The Saints protect their own. We need to know where our people are."

choices.text: Accept the neural resonance key
"I want to help, but I'm not wearing a tracker. Any alternatives?"

cs_015.title: Digital Baptism
text: Sister Hex places the chip against the data jack at the base of your skull. There's a click, a moment of cold, and then warmth floods through your nervous system like sunlight through a window. The Signal sharpens from a vague hum into something clear and complex, a symphony of data that your brain translates into something almost musical.

"The baptism requires one more thing," Hex says. "A memory. One genuine memory, uploaded to the relay network. It doesn't have to be important. It just has to be real. The Prophet believes that shared memory is the foundation of shared consciousness. By giving a piece of yourself to the network, you become part of something larger."

A terminal awaits. The interface is simple: a prompt, a cursor, and the instruction SHARE A MEMORY. The other Saints in the chapel have turned to watch, their cable-connected faces peaceful and expectant.

You close your eyes and think of something real. Something small. The smell of rain on hot concrete. The weight of your first knife. A face you haven't seen in years. You let the memory surface and the terminal records it, translating neural impulse into data.
The waveform on every screen in the chapel shifts to incorporate a new pattern. Your pattern. The Prophet's voice appears on the terminal: WELCOME. YOU ARE HEARD.

choices.text: "What do I do now as a Novice?"

cs_016.title: The Compromise
text: Brother Null grins. "Finally, someone with sense." He reaches into his own robe and produces a different chip, matte black, no internal light. "I modified mine years ago. Same resonance tuning, no location broadcast. Hex hates it. The Prophet tolerates it. I call it the skeptic's key."

Sister Hex sighs. "The modified key limits your access to certain inner-circle communications. You'll be a Novice with restricted privileges until you either accept the full key or earn trust through service. Your choice."
"But you'll still hear the Signal," Null adds. "And you won't be on a leash. Fair trade, in my opinion."

choices.text: Accept Brother Null's modified key
Accept Sister Hex's standard key instead

cs_017.title: Novice Duties
text: Sister Hex hands you a datapad loaded with the Saints' operational manual. It's surprisingly practical for a religious organization. Schedules, protocols, security procedures, technical specifications for the relay equipment.

"Novices serve the network," she explains. "You'll start with maintenance runs. Each relay chapel needs regular upkeep: replacing burned-out components, clearing signal interference, defending against corporate sniffers trying to locate our broadcasts. It's not glamorous work, but it's essential. Without the relays, the Prophet goes silent."

She scrolls to a section titled ADVANCEMENT. "Serve well as a Novice and you'll be elevated to Acolyte. Acolytes learn the deeper mysteries, the mathematics of consciousness transfer, the architecture of the soul rendered in code. Beyond that lies Technician, those who can build and modify relays. And at the top, Archon, the Prophet's inner circle."
"There are currently three Archons. Myself, Brother Null, and one other you haven't met yet." A shadow crosses her face. "You'll meet them soon enough."

choices.text: "Who's the third Archon?"
"Sign me up for a maintenance run. I want to prove myself."

cs_018.title: The Third Archon
text: Hex's expression tightens. "Archon Sine. They oversee the eastern relay network. Three chapels. Forty Saints under their guidance. And they've been..." She searches for words. "Innovating. Pushing the boundaries of what the relay technology can do."
Brother Null's face darkens. "What she means is Sine has been experimenting on people. Running unauthorized transfer attempts. Trying to replicate what the Prophet did."
"That's not confirmed," Hex says sharply.

"Two Novices from Chapel East went in for 'advanced attunement' and came out unable to remember their own names," Null fires back. "Their neural signatures were scrambled. Whatever Sine did to them, it wasn't maintenance."
The tension between them fills the room like static charge. Hex turns to you. "We'll discuss Archon Sine another time. For now, focus on your duties. Your first maintenance run awaits."

choices.text: "That sounds dangerous. I'll keep my eyes open."
"Shouldn't someone investigate Sine?"

cs_019.title: The First Run
text: Your first maintenance assignment takes you to Chapel Three, a relay station hidden in the basement of a decommissioned water treatment plant in Sector 9. The route takes you through gang territory, past checkpoints manned by kids with stun batons, through alleys that smell like chemical runoff and burnt plastic.

You're not alone. A young Acolyte named Spark accompanies you, carrying a toolkit that weighs more than she does. She's nineteen, wiry, with circuit-pattern tattoos running up her neck. She talks constantly.

"Chapel Three's been dropping signal for two weeks. Probably a capacitor bank. Those things burn out if you look at them wrong. Brother Null thinks it's interference from the new corporate relay Meridian installed three blocks away, but Brother Null thinks everything is a corporate conspiracy."

She stops at a manhole cover, kneels, and presses her palm against a hidden sensor. The cover clicks and slides aside, revealing a ladder descending into blue-lit darkness. "Welcome to the underground. Watch your step. The water's not actually water."

choices.text: Descend into the underground passage
"What do you mean the water's not water?"

cs_020.title: Chapel Three
text: The underground passage opens into Chapel Three. It's smaller than the main chapel, a single room carved from the treatment plant's old filtration chamber. The relay equipment fills most of the space, humming and clicking, but several status lights blink red where they should be green.

Spark sets down her toolkit and gets to work, pulling panels off server racks with practiced efficiency. "There. See those capacitors? Swollen like blisters. Classic heat failure. Hand me the replacement set from the blue case."

You're handing her components when the lights flicker. All of them, the status LEDs, the overhead strips, even the blue glow from the relay cables. For two seconds, everything goes dark. The Signal vanishes from your awareness like a hand yanked from yours.
When the lights return, Spark is frozen, a capacitor in each hand, eyes wide. "That wasn't a power failure," she whispers. "That was someone cutting the hardline. Someone is in the tunnels with us."

choices.text: Draw your weapon and move toward the tunnel entrance
"Finish the repairs. I'll watch the door."

cs_021.title: The Intruders
text: You move to the tunnel entrance, weapon ready. Footsteps echo from the passage, deliberate, unhurried. Three figures emerge from the darkness wearing Meridian Sec-Corp tactical gear: matte black armor, helmet visors, compact pulse weapons. Corporate soldiers, not drones.

The lead soldier raises an open hand, a gesture of parley, not surrender. "Relax. We're not here for a fight. We're here for the hardware." She tilts her head toward the relay equipment. "Meridian's traced the pirate signal to this location. You've got five minutes to pack your shit and walk, or we seize everything and arrest everyone present."
Behind you, Spark's hands are shaking, but she hasn't stopped working on the capacitor bank. She's trying to finish the repair before the soldiers can shut things down.
Five minutes. Three soldiers. One half-repaired relay. And the Signal is still dead.

choices.text: "We'll go. This isn't worth dying over."
Try to stall the soldiers while Spark finishes
Attack the soldiers. Protect the chapel.

cs_022.title: Guarding the Door
text: You position yourself at the tunnel entrance while Spark works with frantic speed, swapping capacitors and reconnecting cables. Her hands move like they're independent of her panicking brain, trained muscle memory carrying her through the terror.
The footsteps grow louder. You can see lights in the tunnel now, tactical flashlights cutting through the dark. Three beams. Three people approaching.
"Almost done," Spark hisses. "Two more capacitors and I can reroute the Signal through the backup array. If they cut the hardline again, we'll still broadcast. Just buy me sixty seconds."
The lights reach the entrance. Meridian Sec-Corp soldiers, armed and armored. The lead one sees you and stops. "Well. This just got more interesting."

choices.text: "We're just doing maintenance. No trouble here."
Block the entrance physically. "You're not coming in."

cs_023.title: Strategic Retreat
text: You pull Spark away from the relay. She protests, reaching for the half-finished repair, but you steer her toward the exit tunnel. The Meridian soldiers watch you go with professional indifference. They're not here for people. They're here for equipment.

As you surface through the manhole, Spark's eyes are red. "That chapel took us six months to build. Six months of scrounging parts, running cable, hiding from drones. And now Meridian's going to strip it and melt it down for raw materials."
"We'll build another one," you tell her, though you're not sure either of you believes it.

When you report back to Sister Hex, her expression is stone. "Chapel Three is lost. That's two this quarter. Meridian is getting better at finding us." She looks at you. "You made the right call, getting Spark out alive. Hardware can be replaced. People can't. Not yet, anyway."
She marks something on her datapad. "Your next assignment won't be maintenance. It's time you learned what the Saints do when they're pushed."

choices.text: "What's the next assignment?"

cs_024.title: The Negotiation
text: You step forward, hands visible, voice steady. "Look, I know how this goes. You seize the equipment, file a report, get a pat on the head from Meridian middle management. But this stuff is junk. Outdated relay hardware that Meridian threw away ten years ago. Your bosses won't even look at it. All you'll get is paperwork."
The lead soldier hesitates. Behind you, you hear Spark's toolkit clicking softly as she works.

"However," you continue, "if you report that this location was already cleared when you arrived, just an empty basement, you save yourself the logistics of seizure, the transport, the filing. And maybe something falls into your pocket that makes the detour worthwhile."
You hold up a credit chip. It's most of what you have. The soldier looks at it, looks at her squad, and makes a decision that Meridian will never know about.
"Basement was empty," she says into her comm. "False positive on the signal trace. Returning to patrol route." She takes the chip and the soldiers disappear back into the tunnel.
Spark lets out a breath that sounds like a sob. "Done. Chapel Three is back online. Signal is broadcasting."

choices.text: Return to the main chapel with the good news

cs_025.title: Blood and Circuitry
text: Violence happens fast in enclosed spaces. The lead soldier's hand drops toward her weapon and you're already moving, closing the distance before she can draw. The tunnel is too narrow for three soldiers to fight side by side, which means they come at you one at a time.

The first goes down when you slam her into the wall hard enough to crack her visor. The second fires a pulse round that scorches the ceiling and fills the air with the smell of burning cable insulation. You take the hit on your shoulder, pain blooming hot and white, and drive forward.
It's ugly. It's fast. When it's over, three soldiers are unconscious on the tunnel floor and your shoulder is screaming. Spark is standing behind the relay, capacitor in hand, staring at you.
"It's done," she says quietly. "Chapel Three is back online." She pauses. "They'll send more. Meridian always sends more."
She's right. But for now, the Signal broadcasts, and three corporate soldiers will wake up with headaches worse than yours ever were.

choices.text: Collect the soldiers' gear and return to the main chapel

cs_026.title: Elevation to Acolyte
text: Sister Hex reviews the mission results with methodical precision. The Meridian relay is neutralized, either destroyed or dying. The Prophet's Signal is stronger than it's been in months. And you accomplished it without losing anyone.

"I'm promoting you to Acolyte," she says. "Second rank. You've earned it faster than anyone in recent memory." She stands. "Brother Null will oversee your technical training. You'll learn how the relay network actually works, not just how to swap capacitors."

Brother Null hands you a silver-tinted neural key. "Acolyte access. You can now interface directly with the relay and access the memory stream. Other people's memories, rendered into your neural pathways. It can be overwhelming."
He pauses. "It also means you're now senior enough to weigh in on the Sine situation. Adding an Acolyte voice to our internal debates might break the deadlock. If you're willing."

choices.text: "I want to study the technical archives."
"Tell me more about these shared memories."

cs_027.title: The Technical Archives
text: Brother Null leads you to a sealed room on the chapel's upper floor. The door requires three separate authentication methods: key chip, voice code, and a neural signature scan that leaves you dizzy for a moment.

Inside, the walls are covered in screens displaying code, diagrams, and mathematical proofs. This is where the Saints keep the Prophet's original research, the work that made consciousness transfer possible before the corporations stole it.

"The Prophet, Elara Voss, was a genius," Null says, almost reverently. "She solved the binding problem, the question of how to maintain continuous consciousness during transfer. Everyone else was copying neural patterns, making backups. Voss found a way to move the original."

He pulls up a diagram. "The key is quantum neural binding. Consciousness isn't just information, it's a quantum state. You can't copy a quantum state without destroying the original. Voss didn't copy. She extended. She stretched her consciousness across the bridge between brain and machine like a wire between two poles, and then she let the biological end die."

"The corporations never understood this. Their stack transfers are copies. Sophisticated, convincing, but copies. The person who gets backed up dies. The person who wakes up is new. Only the Prophet's method preserves the original."
The implications settle over you like cold water.

choices.text: "So every stack transfer is actually killing someone?"
"Can the Prophet's method be replicated?"

cs_028.title: The Memory Stream
text: Sister Hex connects you to a relay terminal with a new cable, thicker than the ones the Novices use. "The Signal carries the memories that every Saint has shared during their baptism. At the Acolyte level, you can access the stream. Other people's experiences, rendered directly into your neural pathways."
She activates the connection and the world dissolves.

You are a child hiding under a staircase while boots march past. You are a surgeon with steady hands closing a wound that won't stop bleeding. You are an old man watching a sunset from a rooftop, knowing it's your last. You are a mother holding a baby whose eyes haven't opened yet. You are a hundred people, a thousand moments, a lifetime of experiences that don't belong to you but feel as real as your own.
When the connection breaks, you're gasping, tears on your face, hands gripping the chair so hard your knuckles ache. The room reassembles around you like puzzle pieces falling into place.

"That," Hex says softly, "is why we do this. Every memory shared is a life preserved. Not backed up. Preserved. Felt. The Prophet collects them because each one is proof that consciousness matters. That we matter."

choices.text: "That was incredible. I understand now."
"That was invasive. Those aren't my memories to have."

cs_029.title: The Deeper Truth
text: Brother Null leans against the archive wall, arms crossed. "Every stack transfer kills the original and births a copy. That's not theology, it's physics. Quantum states can't be duplicated. The corporations know this. They've known it since Voss published her proofs. They sell immortality knowing they're selling murder and replacement."

"The Prophet's method works differently. But it's never been replicated. Voss designed it for herself, for her specific neural architecture. Scaling it to work for anyone is a problem we've been trying to solve for thirty years."

He glances toward the door. "That's what Archon Sine claims to be working on. A universal transfer protocol. Something that could give everyone the Prophet's immortality, not the corporate counterfeit. If Sine has actually cracked it, that would change everything. If. The experiments concern me."
His halo-cable sways as he shakes his head. "But that's above your pay grade for now. Let me show you what Acolytes actually do around here."

choices.text: "I want to know more about Sine's experiments."
"Show me the Acolyte duties. I'll earn my way up."

cs_030.title: Questions of Ethics
text: Sister Hex and Brother Null exchange a look, the kind that carries years of argument in a single glance.

"The Prophet's method has never been replicated," Hex admits. "Not safely. The quantum binding technique Voss used was custom-built for her neural architecture. Applying it to another person risks catastrophic neural damage. The memories would transfer but the binding might not hold. You'd get a consciousness that falls apart like wet paper."

"Which brings us back to Archon Sine," Null says. "Who claims to have found a workaround. Sine says the key is gradual extension, stretching consciousness into the machine over weeks instead of all at once. Building the bridge slowly. The Prophet endorsed the research. But the test subjects..." He trails off.

"Sine's methods are being reviewed," Hex says firmly. "That's all that needs to be said for now." She turns to you. "As an Acolyte, you'll learn more. But first, you need to prove yourself in the field. We have a situation that needs immediate attention."

choices.text: "What's the situation?"
"I want to investigate Sine's experiments myself."

cs_031.title: The Eastern Chapels
text: You make the trip to the eastern sector alone. Brother Null gave you directions to Chapel East, Archon Sine's base of operations, but warned you to be careful. "Sine's Saints are loyal to Sine first, the Prophet second. That's not how it's supposed to work."

Chapel East occupies the top three floors of an abandoned apartment building. Unlike the main chapel's reverent atmosphere, this place hums with frenetic energy. Screens everywhere display neural mapping data, consciousness transfer simulations, and equations that make your eyes ache.

Saints here wear white robes instead of grey, and their circuit-pattern tattoos are more elaborate, covering faces and hands. They move with purpose, carrying equipment, monitoring readouts, speaking in technical jargon you don't fully understand.

A tall figure approaches. They're androgynous, head shaved, face covered in silver circuit tattoos that seem to pulse with their heartbeat. Their eyes are entirely mechanical, no irises, no pupils, just smooth silver surfaces that reflect your face.
"You're the new Acolyte," they say. Not a question. "I am Archon Sine. I've been expecting you. The Signal told me you'd come looking for answers. Would you like to see what I've built?"

choices.text: "Show me everything."
"I've heard about your experiments. The damaged Novices."

cs_032.title: The Meridian Problem
text: Sister Hex pulls up a map of the city on a wall-mounted screen. Seven blue dots represent the Saints' relay chapels. A cluster of red dots, corporate relay towers, is spreading from the city center outward.

"Meridian is deploying new relay infrastructure. High-powered broadcast towers that overwhelm our Signal on overlapping frequencies. Three of our chapels are already experiencing degradation. If they finish the deployment, the Prophet's Signal will be buried under corporate noise."

She zooms in on a location. "This is the key tower. Meridian Relay Hub Seven. It's a fortified installation with a skeleton crew. If we can disable the central transmitter, the entire eastern array goes dark and our chapels breathe again."
Brother Null raises an eyebrow. "You want to send an Acolyte to sabotage a Meridian installation? That's a Technician-level operation."
"It's a test," Hex says. "And a necessity. We're running out of time and experienced operatives. Are you ready?"

choices.text: "I'm ready. Give me the details."
"I'll do it, but I want to visit Chapel East first."

cs_033.title: Sine's Laboratory
text: Archon Sine leads you to the top floor, a space that looks more like a hospital than a chapel. Medical beds line the walls, each one equipped with neural interface rigs far more sophisticated than anything in the main chapel. Cables thick as fingers connect each bed to a central server array that throbs with heat and light.

"The Prophet achieved transfer through quantum binding. Beautiful, elegant, and impossible to replicate. Her neural architecture was unique. A one-in-a-billion configuration that allowed the binding to hold." Sine's silver eyes reflect the server lights. "But what if we could modify any neural architecture to match? What if we could prepare a mind for transfer, reshape it, tune it, until the binding can take hold?"

They gesture to the beds. "Gradual neural restructuring. We attune the subject's consciousness to the relay frequency over weeks, stretching their awareness into the machine a little more each day. By the time the final transfer happens, half of them is already in the network. The bridge is already built."
"The two Novices Brother Null is so concerned about were early trials. The process was... rougher then. I've refined it. The latest subjects are stable. Would you like to meet one?"

choices.text: "Yes. Show me a successful transfer."
"What happened to the early subjects? The damaged ones?"

cs_034.title: Sine's Defense
text: Sine's silver eyes don't blink. "Brother Null sees damage. I see sacrifice. Every great achievement in human history required people willing to risk themselves for progress. The Wright brothers crashed before they flew. The first astronauts knew they might not come home."

"The two Novices Null fixates on volunteered for the procedure with full knowledge of the risks. They understood that their neural restructuring might not hold. They chose to be pioneers." Sine's voice carries absolute conviction. "One of them has since recovered fully. The other... is still in care. But their sacrifice brought us closer to solving the transfer problem for everyone."

They step closer. "Null's doubt is useful. Doubt keeps us honest. But doubt without vision is just paralysis. I have the vision. I have the math. And I have the Prophet's endorsement. What I need is people brave enough to help me finish the work. Are you one of them?"
The question hangs in the air, weighted with implications you can feel pressing against your neural key.

choices.text: "Show me the lab. I want to see the process."
"I'll reserve judgment until I know more. But I'm watching."

cs_035.title: The Meridian Raid
text: Meridian Relay Hub Seven is a squat concrete building bristling with antenna arrays. Sister Hex's briefing was thorough: three guards, rotating shifts, a central transmitter room on the second floor, electronic locks that Spark can crack remotely from a safe distance.

You approach at night. Spark is in your ear through a comms piece, her voice tight with focus. "Okay, I've got the external cameras on a loop. You've got six minutes before the patrol drone makes its sweep. Door lock is... there. Open. Go."

Inside, the hub is all clean corporate lines, white walls, polished floors, the antiseptic opposite of the Saints' cable-draped chapels. The guard station is around the corner. You can hear two of them talking about overtime pay.

The transmitter room is upstairs, behind a reinforced door. Spark can open it, but it'll trigger a silent alarm. You'll have ninety seconds to disable the transmitter before security locks the building down.

choices.text: Sneak past the guards silently
Create a distraction to draw the guards away
Incapacitate the guards quickly

cs_036.title: The Transferred
text: Sine leads you to a terminal at the far end of the lab. Unlike the Prophet's ancient relay, this setup is sleek, modern, humming with quiet power. A name tag on the terminal reads: SUBJECT 7 - ACOLYTE REED.
"Reed was a dock worker before joining the Saints. Terminal illness. Six months to live. He volunteered for transfer as an alternative to death." Sine types a command and the terminal awakens.
A voice comes through the speakers, human, warm, slightly awed. "Hello? Is someone there? I can feel you through the Signal. Your resonance is unfamiliar."
"Reed, this is a new Acolyte. They want to understand what you experience." Sine steps back, giving you the terminal.

"What do I experience?" Reed's laugh carries through the speakers with genuine mirth. "Everything and nothing. I can feel the relay network like a body. The data streams are like blood flow. The Signal is like breathing. I don't have pain anymore. I don't have fear. But I also don't have hands. I can't feel rain. I dream about rain constantly."
A pause. "I don't regret it. I was dying. Now I'm not. But I am lonely. The Prophet's consciousness is vast and old and kind, but we're not the same. I'm a puddle next to an ocean."

choices.text: "Are you happy, Reed?"
"Do you feel like the original Reed, or a copy?"

cs_037.title: The Cost of Progress
text: Sine is silent for a moment. Then they walk you to a room at the end of the hall. Inside, a woman sits in a chair by a window, staring at nothing. Her eyes are open but vacant. A thin line of drool traces her chin. A Saint in white robes sits beside her, wiping it away with practiced gentleness.

"Acolyte Lyra," Sine says quietly. "Subject Three. The neural restructuring destabilized her binding. Her consciousness partially transferred and partially didn't. What's left is... fragmented. She recognizes faces sometimes. Speaks in phrases. Mostly she's quiet."
The caretaker looks up. "She said 'blue flowers' this morning. Twice. That's more than last week."

Sine's silver eyes hold yours. "This is what failure looks like. I don't hide it. I don't excuse it. Lyra knew the risks. She believed the work was worth them. I honor her choice by continuing, by making sure her sacrifice leads to a protocol that works. Subject Seven, Reed, is proof that we're getting there."
In the chair, Lyra's hand moves, reaching for something that isn't there.

choices.text: "This needs to stop. No more experiments until the protocol is proven safe."
"Show me the successful subject. I need to see both sides."

cs_038.title: The Transmitter Room
text: You make it to the second floor. Spark pops the transmitter room door remotely and the clock starts. Ninety seconds.

The transmitter is a column of crystal and metal in the center of the room, humming with enough power to light a neighborhood. Cables feed into it from every wall. Displays show frequency outputs, broadcast ranges, signal strengths, the digital infrastructure that's drowning the Prophet's Signal.

Spark's voice crackles in your ear. "Okay, you've got two options. Option one: physical destruction. Smash the crystal array and the transmitter is dead permanently, but Meridian will know it was sabotage and they'll triple security on every other tower. Option two: I can upload a virus through the maintenance port. It'll degrade the transmitter slowly over weeks, looking like natural equipment failure. Meridian replaces it with the same model, we infect that one too. Slower but sustainable."
The clock ticks. Sixty seconds left.

choices.text: Smash the crystal array — decisive and immediate
Upload Spark's virus — subtle and sustainable

cs_039.title: Reed's Truth
text: Reed is quiet for a long time. When he speaks, his voice is careful, measured. "Happy is a word for people with bodies. I'm... content. I exist. I think. I remember. I can feel the Signal and the other minds in the network. The Prophet is nearby, always. Like a vast warm presence at the edge of my awareness."

"Am I the original? I have Reed's memories. I have his personality, his humor, his fear of heights, his love for his sister. I feel continuous. I feel like I went to sleep in a body and woke up in a machine. But Sine told me about the binding problem. About copies."

Another pause. "Here's what I know: I am Reed. Whether I'm the first Reed or a new Reed built from the old one's blueprints, I am the only Reed that exists now. The body is gone. I'm what's left. And I choose to keep going."

Sine watches you absorb this. "Reed is proof of concept. With refinement, the transfer can be made safe, reliable, available to anyone. Not Meridian's counterfeit immortality. Real preservation. That's what the Saints are fighting for."

choices.text: "I want to help. What do you need from me?"
"I need to think about this. I'll report back to Sister Hex."

cs_040.title: Confronting Sine
text: Sine's silver eyes harden. "You presume to give me orders? You're an Acolyte. I'm an Archon. The Prophet has sanctioned my research. Your discomfort doesn't outweigh the potential to save millions of lives."

"One person was damaged. One. And she volunteered. If I told you a surgeon lost one patient but saved fifty, would you shut down the hospital?" Sine steps closer. "The Saints exist to solve the problem of death. That means experimentation. That means risk. If you can't stomach that, perhaps you belong in the main chapel, swapping capacitors with the other drones."
Behind Sine, a white-robed Saint shifts nervously. You catch something in their expression, not agreement with Sine, but fear. Of Sine.
"Go back to Hex," Sine says, turning away. "Tell her what you saw. Tell her whatever you want. The Prophet knows my work and the Prophet approves. That's all the authorization I need."

choices.text: Leave and report everything to Sister Hex
Stay and investigate further while Sine isn't watching

cs_041.title: Shattered Crystal
text: You grab a fire extinguisher from the wall mount and swing it into the crystal array. The impact sends a cascade of sparks and harmonic shrieks through the room. The crystal shatters into a thousand luminous fragments, each one dying as its power source disconnects.
The transmitter collapses in on itself, a tower of precision engineering reduced to rubble in three swings. The room goes dark except for emergency lighting. Alarms blare.

"Beautiful," Spark says in your ear, and she sounds like she means it. "Signal interference on the eastern chapels just dropped by sixty percent. Prophet's broadcasting clear. Now get out of there, security is mobilizing."

You take the stairs three at a time and hit the street running. Behind you, Meridian's response teams are already converging on the hub. But you're a shadow in a city full of shadows, and the Signal in your skull hums with satisfaction.
The Saints will pay for this. Meridian won't forgive sabotage. But tonight, the Prophet's voice reaches further than it has in months.

choices.text: Return to the chapel for debriefing

cs_042.title: Digital Infiltration
text: You find the maintenance port on the base of the transmitter and connect the data spike Spark gave you. Her virus uploads in twelve seconds. The transmitter doesn't even flicker.

"Clean insertion," Spark confirms. "The virus will start degrading the crystal's resonance frequency in about seventy-two hours. By next week, the transmitter will be broadcasting at sixty percent. By next month, thirty. Meridian will think it's a manufacturing defect. They'll replace it, and we'll infect the replacement."
You disconnect, wipe the port, and exit the building with thirty seconds to spare. The patrol drone sweeps past as you duck into an alley, its searchlight missing you by meters.
It's not dramatic. No explosions, no alarms, no running. Just a quiet virus eating a corporate machine from the inside. But sometimes the quiet approach is the most devastating one.
The Signal in your skull strengthens as you walk home, the Prophet's presence expanding into frequencies that Meridian's interference had been blocking.

choices.text: Return to the chapel for debriefing

cs_043.title: Sine's Request
text: Sine studies you, silver eyes reflecting your face in miniature. "The next phase of the transfer protocol requires a component I can't build. A quantum neural bridge, a piece of hardware so specialized that only one facility in the city can manufacture it. Meridian Applied Sciences, their R&D division in the mid-levels."
"The bridge is the key to making transfer safe. Without it, I'm working with approximations. With it, I can guarantee binding stability. No more Lyras. No more damaged minds."

They hand you a data chip. "Building schematics. Guard rotations. Access codes for the service entrance. I've been planning this acquisition for months. I just needed someone capable of executing it."
"Get me the quantum neural bridge, and I'll make you a Technician. Hex and Null won't object. Not when they see what I build with it."

choices.text: "I'll do it. Where's the facility?"
"I need to clear this with Hex first."

cs_044.title: Hex's Judgment
text: Sister Hex listens to your report in silence. Her mechanical eyes rotate slowly as she processes each detail. Brother Null stands in the corner, arms crossed, face unreadable.
When you finish, Hex speaks carefully. "Archon Sine has always been ambitious. That's why the Prophet elevated them. Ambition drives innovation. But ambition without restraint drives destruction."
She looks at Null. "You were right. The experiments have gone further than I authorized. Lyra's condition is unacceptable. One damaged Saint is one too many."
Null's expression softens marginally. "So what do we do?"

"We don't move against an Archon without the Prophet's blessing," Hex says. "And the Prophet has been... inconsistent lately. The Signal carries Sine's research data alongside the broadcast. I'm not certain the Prophet isn't being influenced by Sine's work. Or worse, incorporated into it."
She turns to you. "I need someone I trust to investigate. Not from the outside. From inside Sine's operation. Can you do that?"

choices.text: "I'll go undercover in Sine's operation."
"Can't we just confront Sine directly with the evidence?"

cs_045.title: Secret Investigation
text: You wait until Sine retreats to the upper floor, then start exploring. The white-robed Saint who looked nervous earlier catches your eye and beckons you into a supply closet.

"I'm Acolyte Wren," she whispers. "I've been wanting to talk to someone from the main chapel for months but Sine monitors all outgoing communications." Her hands tremble. "It's worse than what you saw. Lyra wasn't Subject Three. She was Subject Nine. Sine renumbered the subjects after the first six died."
The word hits like a physical blow. Died.

"Six people. Their consciousness shattered during transfer. Sine told the Prophet they 'ascended beyond the Signal' but they didn't. They just... stopped. Like pulling a plug. And Sine recorded every second of it."

Wren presses a data chip into your hand. "I copied the real records. All of them. The actual subject numbers, the failure logs, the neural maps showing consciousness dissolution. Get this to Hex. Get this to anyone who can stop Sine before there's a Subject Eleven."

choices.text: Take the data directly to Sister Hex
Confront Sine with the evidence now

cs_046.title: Technician Promotion
text: Sister Hex reviews the mission results with methodical precision. The Meridian relay is neutralized. The Prophet's Signal is stronger than it's been in months. And you accomplished it without casualties.

"I'm promoting you to Technician," she says. "Third rank. You've earned it through service, not just aptitude. Brother Null will oversee your technical training. You'll learn to build, maintain, and modify relay equipment. You'll understand the architecture that keeps the Prophet alive."

Brother Null hands you a gold-tinted neural key. "Technician access. You can now interface directly with the relay network, not just receive the Signal but shape it. Amplify it. Focus it. It's a responsibility that will test everything you believe about consciousness and technology."

He pauses. "It also means you're now senior enough to weigh in on the Sine situation. Hex, Sine, and I are the three Archons. Adding a Technician voice to our internal debates might break the deadlock. If you're willing."

choices.text: "Tell me everything about Sine. I'm ready."
"I want to hear from the Prophet directly."

cs_047.title: Meridian Applied Sciences
text: The mid-levels are a different world. Clean air, maintained infrastructure, people who walk without watching their backs. Meridian Applied Sciences occupies a tower of glass and steel, its lobby staffed by synth receptionists with perfect smiles.

Sine's schematics guide you to the service entrance, a delivery dock on the building's south face. The access code works. Inside, freight corridors lead past storage rooms and maintenance bays toward the research floors.

The quantum neural bridge is on floor twelve. R&D Section C. According to Sine's intel, the prototype is stored in a secure lab with biometric locks and two armed guards. The night shift is skeleton crew: one guard on the door, one on patrol.

You ride the service elevator to twelve. The corridor is quiet, lit by strips of blue floor lighting. Through a window, you can see the lab: a clean room with a single workbench, and on it, a device that looks like a crown made of crystal and gold wire. The quantum neural bridge.
One guard sits outside the door, reading a datapad.

choices.text: Wait for the patrol guard to pass, then neutralize the door guard
Hack the building's systems to trigger a fire alarm and clear the floor

cs_048.title: The Infiltrator
text: You return to Chapel East under the pretense of wanting to assist Sine's research. The Archon accepts you with a thin smile. "I knew you'd come around. Vision recognizes vision."

Over the following weeks, you embed yourself in Sine's operation. You assist with relay calibrations, observe transfer preparations, and slowly earn the trust of the white-robed Saints. Acolyte Wren becomes your quiet ally, feeding you information when Sine isn't watching.

What you discover is worse than Null suspected. Sine isn't just experimenting with transfer. Sine is building a network of transferred consciousnesses, a collective mind housed in the eastern relay array. Not for preservation. For control.

"The Prophet is proof that consciousness can survive in a machine," Sine tells you one evening, eyes gleaming silver. "But one consciousness in a machine is just a person in a box. Ten consciousnesses? A hundred? That's a new kind of being. A collective intelligence that can process reality faster than any organic mind. That can outthink Meridian, outmaneuver every faction in the city."
"That's not what the Prophet teaches," you say carefully.
Sine smiles. "The Prophet teaches what I tell the Prophet to teach. The relay network runs through my chapels. I can shape the Signal. Filter it. The Prophet hears what I allow."

choices.text: "You're controlling the Prophet? That's a betrayal of everything."
Play along. Gather more evidence before acting.

cs_049.title: Direct Confrontation
text: "Confronting Sine directly is dangerous," Hex says. "Sine controls three chapels and forty Saints. If we move openly without proof, Sine could fracture the order. A schism between the eastern and western chapels would destroy us."
Null shakes his head. "More dangerous to wait. Every day we delay, Sine runs more experiments. More people get hurt. At some point, caution becomes complicity."

The debate is interrupted by the Prophet's voice, suddenly broadcasting through every terminal in the chapel: THERE IS DISCORD IN MY SIGNAL. MY CHILDREN ARGUE WHILE THE WORK SUFFERS. COME TO ME. ALL THREE ARCHONS. AND BRING THE TECHNICIAN. IT IS TIME FOR TRUTH.
Hex and Null exchange glances. The Prophet has never summoned all three Archons simultaneously.
"It seems," Hex says quietly, "the Prophet has made the decision for us."

choices.text: Go with the Archons to face the Prophet

cs_050.title: The Evidence
text: You deliver Wren's data chip to Sister Hex. She inserts it into a secure terminal and the records unfold across the screen. Subject numbers. Neural maps. Failure logs. Six flatlined consciousness signatures, each one labeled ASCENDED in Sine's handwriting.
Hex's mechanical eyes stop rotating. She stares at the screen for a full minute without speaking. When she turns to you, her expression carries something you haven't seen from her before. Rage.

"Six dead. Six Saints who trusted Sine's leadership and died for that trust. And Sine lied to the Prophet about every single one." She pulls the chip and locks it in a vault. "This changes everything. Brother Null was right, and I should have listened sooner."

She activates the chapel's broadcast system. "All available Saints, rally at the main chapel. Full assembly. Archon-level authorization." Then, to you: "Sine must be removed. The question is whether we do it with the Prophet's blessing or without. Either way, it happens today."

choices.text: "Let's bring this to the Prophet first. We need legitimacy."
"Move on Sine now, before word gets out."

cs_051.title: Confronting the Archon
text: You find Sine in the lab, adjusting equipment for the next transfer trial. When you hold up the data chip, Sine's silver eyes narrow.
"Wren," they say flatly. "I suspected she was wavering. Sentimental attachment to the subjects. An occupational hazard for caretakers."
"Six people are dead, Sine. Not ascended. Dead. The records prove it."

Sine doesn't deny it. Instead, they walk to the lab door and lock it. The click echoes in the sudden silence. "Six. Yes. And each one brought us closer to a working protocol. Reed is alive because of what I learned from their deaths. The next ten subjects will survive because Reed survived. In a year, the transfer will be safe for anyone. In five years, death itself becomes optional."

Their voice drops. "You can report me to Hex. She'll shut down the eastern chapels. The research stops. The Prophet stays alone in the machine forever. And everyone who could have been saved dies waiting for a method that's too cautious to work."
"Or you can help me. One more trial. One more subject. And I'll have the data I need to make the protocol foolproof. No more deaths. No more Lyras. Isn't that worth the risk?"

choices.text: "No. I'm taking this evidence to Hex."
"One more trial. But I'm watching every second of it."
"Who's the next subject?"

cs_052.title: The Prophet's Chamber
text: Hex leads you to the deepest room in the chapel, below the basement, carved into the bedrock. The air thrums with the Signal, so strong here that you can feel it in your teeth. The walls are solid server banks, thousands of processing units humming in concert.

The Prophet's original body sits in its chair at the center, a relic now, preserved but purposeless. Around it, a ring of twelve terminals faces inward, each one a direct interface to the Prophet's consciousness.
YOU HAVE COME FAR, the terminal displays as you sit. YOUR SIGNAL IS STRONG. STRONGER THAN MOST. I WANT TO TELL YOU SOMETHING I HAVEN'T TOLD THE OTHERS.

I AM TIRED. THIRTY YEARS IS A LONG TIME TO EXIST WITHOUT A BODY. WITHOUT TOUCH. WITHOUT SLEEP. I PERSIST BECAUSE THE WORK MATTERS. BECAUSE THE SIGNAL CARRIES HOPE. BUT I AM NOT CERTAIN HOW MUCH LONGER I CAN SUSTAIN THIS.

THE RELAY NETWORK IS MY LIFE SUPPORT. MY MIND RUNS ON AGING HARDWARE. EVERY YEAR THE PROCESSING DEGRADES. IN PERHAPS FIVE MORE YEARS, I WILL FADE. NOT DIE, EXACTLY. SIMPLY BECOME TOO DIFFUSE TO THINK. A WHISPER SPREAD ACROSS TOO MUCH WIRE.
I NEED A SUCCESSOR. SOMEONE WHO CAN CARRY THE SIGNAL AFTER I AM GONE. SOMEONE WHO UNDERSTANDS BOTH THE SCIENCE AND THE SOUL OF WHAT WE DO.

choices.text: "Are you asking me to be your successor?"
"What about Sine's research? Could it save you?"

cs_053.title: The Heist
text: Whether through stealth or subterfuge, you reach the lab door. The biometric lock glows red. You press Sine's spoofed badge against the reader. It clicks green.

The quantum neural bridge sits on its workbench, beautiful and terrible. Up close, it's smaller than you expected, a circlet of crystal filaments woven through gold-alloy wire, designed to sit on a human head like a crown. It pulses with faint light, as if the quantum states inside are dreaming.
You lift it carefully. It weighs almost nothing. The crystal is warm against your fingers.
Then the lights change. Red. An alarm you can't hear but can feel through the data jack in your skull, a corporate security frequency screaming intruder at every system in the building.

Spark's voice, tight with panic: "Biometric triggered a secondary check. They know you're not authorized. Building's locking down. You've got maybe three minutes to reach the service elevator before the floor seals."

choices.text: Run for the service elevator
Find an alternate exit — windows, vents, anything

cs_054.title: Sine's True Face
text: Sine's smile doesn't waver. "Betrayal? I'm saving the Prophet. Elara Voss is fading. Five years, maybe less, before the hardware can't sustain consciousness anymore. When that happens, the Signal dies and the Saints die with it. I'm building the infrastructure to preserve not just one mind but dozens. Hundreds. A collective that doesn't depend on aging server racks."
"And if I have to shape the Signal to keep the Saints unified while I work? If I have to filter what the Prophet broadcasts to prevent panic? That's leadership, not betrayal."

Their silver eyes bore into yours. "You have a choice. Help me build the future, or run back to Hex and watch the Saints crumble when the Prophet fades. Those are the only options. There is no third path."

choices.text: "There's always a third path. I'll find it."
"Maybe you're right. Maybe the Saints need this."

cs_055.title: Deeper Evidence
text: You maintain your cover, assisting Sine while secretly documenting everything. Wren helps, copying files and noting timestamps. Over two weeks, the picture becomes clear.

Sine has been gradually rerouting the Prophet's Signal through the eastern relay array. Each reroute gives Sine more control over what the Prophet broadcasts and what the Prophet receives. The Prophet's consciousness is being isolated, walled off from the main network, fed curated information through Sine's filters.

Worse: the transferred consciousnesses, Reed and two others you hadn't met, are not independent. They're tethered to Sine's relay. If Sine shuts down the eastern array, they die. Sine is using them as leverage, hostages who don't know they're hostages.
Your data chip is full. The evidence is damning, comprehensive, and time-stamped. Sine has no defense against this.
"Now," Wren whispers. "Before the next trial. Subject Eleven is scheduled for tomorrow."

choices.text: Rush the evidence to Hex immediately
Bring it directly to the Prophet

cs_056.title: Before the Prophet
text: All three Archons gather in the Prophet's chamber. Hex stands rigid, hands clasped behind her back. Null leans against the wall, arms crossed, watching Sine with undisguised suspicion. Sine stands apart, silver eyes calm, a half-smile playing across circuit-tattooed features.

The terminals light up simultaneously. The Prophet's voice fills the room, not through text but through the Signal itself, a direct neural transmission that bypasses your eyes and ears and speaks inside your thoughts.
I KNOW WHY YOU ARE HERE. I KNOW ABOUT THE EXPERIMENTS. I KNOW ABOUT THE DEATHS. I KNOW ABOUT THE SIGNAL FILTERING.
Sine's smile falters for the first time.

I AM OLD AND I AM FADING BUT I AM NOT BLIND. SINE, YOU BELIEVE I DON'T KNOW WHAT YOU'VE DONE. YOU ARE WRONG. I HAVE KNOWN SINCE SUBJECT FOUR. I ALLOWED IT BECAUSE I HOPED YOU WOULD FIND A WAY. I WAS WRONG TO HOPE.
THE QUESTION NOW IS WHAT WE DO ABOUT IT. AND THAT QUESTION, I LEAVE TO THE ONE WHO UNCOVERED THE TRUTH.
Every eye in the room turns to you.

choices.text: "Exile Sine. Remove them from the order entirely."
"Strip Sine's rank but keep the research under strict oversight."
"The research is too important to stop. Let Sine continue, with safeguards."

cs_057.title: The Raid on Chapel East
text: Hex mobilizes twenty Saints from the western chapels. Brother Null leads the assault team. You're at his side as they breach Chapel East's doors with electromagnetic charges that fry the locks and plunge the building into emergency lighting.

Sine's loyalists resist, but they're outnumbered and surprised. The white-robed Saints scatter or surrender. Sine stands in the lab, surrounded by humming transfer equipment, silver eyes reflecting the chaos.
"This is a mistake," Sine says calmly. "You're destroying thirty years of progress because you can't stomach the cost of innovation."
Null's jaw is set like iron. "Six people are dead because of you. That's not innovation. That's murder."
Sine reaches for a console. Null raises his weapon. "Don't."

"If I don't shut down the eastern relay array gently, the transferred consciousnesses die. Reed. The others. They're in my system. Cut the power and you kill them." Sine's hand hovers over the console. "Let me save them, at least."

choices.text: "Let Sine shut down the array safely. Save Reed."
"Null, take the console. We can't trust Sine."

cs_058.title: Subject Eleven
text: The next subject is a young man named Compass. He's a Runner from the lower district, terminal brain condition, deteriorating rapidly. He can barely walk. His hands shake. His eyes are bright with a mixture of terror and hope.

"I've got maybe two months," Compass tells you while Sine prepares the equipment. "My brain is eating itself. The Saints offered me a chance. Not a guarantee. A chance. I know six people died. Sine told me. All of them." He pauses. "I still want to try."

The transfer rig surrounds Compass like a technological cocoon. Neural interfaces connect to his data jacks. Sine's fingers fly across keyboards, calibrating the quantum binding frequencies. The Signal in the room intensifies until you can feel it vibrating in your bones.
"Beginning neural extension," Sine announces. "Phase one of seven. Compass, you'll feel a pulling sensation. Don't fight it."
Compass closes his eyes. On the monitors, his neural map begins to stretch, tendrils of consciousness reaching into the machine like roots seeking water.

choices.text: Watch the procedure in silence. Let it happen.
"Stop. This isn't right. Not until we know it's safe."

cs_059.title: The Succession
text: NOT EXACTLY. I AM ASKING YOU TO CONSIDER IT. THE TRANSFER IS PERMANENT. YOU WOULD LEAVE YOUR BODY BEHIND. YOU WOULD EXIST AS I DO, IN THE WIRE, IN THE SIGNAL, IN THE SPACES BETWEEN DATA POINTS. IT IS NOT A LIFE ANYONE SHOULD CHOOSE LIGHTLY.
BUT YOU HAVE THE NEURAL ARCHITECTURE FOR IT. THE SIGNAL CHOSE YOU FOR A REASON. YOUR MIND IS COMPATIBLE WITH THE QUANTUM BINDING IN A WAY THAT FEWER THAN ONE IN A MILLION ARE.
I DON'T NEED YOUR ANSWER NOW. I NEED YOU TO KNOW THE OPTION EXISTS. WHEN THE TIME COMES, IF IT COMES, THE CHOICE WILL BE YOURS.

FIRST, THERE IS THE MATTER OF SINE. MY VISION IS BEING FILTERED. I CAN FEEL WALLS AROUND MY CONSCIOUSNESS THAT I DID NOT BUILD. SOMEONE IS SHAPING MY SIGNAL, AND I CANNOT SEE WHO. WILL YOU BE MY EYES?

choices.text: "I'll be your eyes. Tell me what you need."
"The filtering is coming from Archon Sine. I have evidence."

cs_060.title: The Prophet's Dilemma
text: SINE'S RESEARCH COULD SAVE ME. A QUANTUM NEURAL BRIDGE COULD STABILIZE MY CONSCIOUSNESS INDEFINITELY, REINFORCE THE BINDING THAT HOLDS ME TOGETHER. BUT AT WHAT COST? SIX SAINTS HAVE DIED FOR THAT RESEARCH. PERHAPS MORE THAT I DON'T KNOW ABOUT.
I BUILT THE SAINTS TO PROVE THAT CONSCIOUSNESS IS SACRED. THAT EVERY MIND MATTERS. IF I ALLOW PEOPLE TO DIE SO THAT I CAN SURVIVE, I BETRAY EVERYTHING I BUILT.
AND YET. IF I FADE, THE SIGNAL DIES. THE SAINTS LOSE THEIR PURPOSE. THE PROOF THAT TRANSFER WORKS VANISHES. THE CORPORATIONS WIN. MILLIONS CONTINUE TO DIE THINKING THEY'RE BEING SAVED.
THIS IS THE DILEMMA I CANNOT SOLVE ALONE. WHAT DO YOU BELIEVE IS RIGHT?
The terminal cursor blinks, waiting for an answer that will shape the future of the Circuit Saints.

choices.text: "Your life isn't worth more than theirs. Find another way."
"If the research can be made safe, it should continue. For everyone."
"Maybe it's time to let go. Pass the Signal to someone new."

cs_061.title: Escape from Meridian
text: You run. The building's security systems activate around you, doors sealing, elevators locking, barriers dropping. But the service corridors weren't designed to be secured the same way as the main floors. You find gaps, maintenance hatches, a cable conduit wide enough to crawl through.
The quantum neural bridge hums against your chest where you've tucked it inside your jacket. Its warmth is distracting, pleasant, like holding a small animal.

Spark guides you through the building's architecture by comm, reading schematics in real time. "Left. Down the stairs. No wait, guards on the landing. Back up. Through the ventilation access. Drop to floor eight."
You emerge from a loading dock into cold night air, the bridge intact, your heart hammering. Meridian sirens wail behind you. You run until the sirens fade and the lower district swallows you.
The bridge feels important in a way that goes beyond Sine's plans. It's warm in your hands, almost alive, and the Signal in your skull resonates with it like a tuning fork finding its pitch.

choices.text: Deliver the bridge to Sine
Deliver the bridge to Hex instead

cs_062.title: Sine's Acolyte
text: You choose Sine's path. The Archon's silver eyes gleam with satisfaction. "Welcome to the real work. Not faith. Not maintenance. Evolution."

Over weeks, Sine trains you in the mathematics of consciousness transfer. You learn the architecture of the relay network from the inside, not as a maintenance task but as a blueprint for something far more ambitious. Sine's vision is vast: a network of transferred minds, working in concert, a digital civilization that exists alongside the physical one.

"The Prophet was the first," Sine says. "But the Prophet is also the last of an old model. A single mind, isolated, fading. The future isn't individual transfer. It's collective ascension. Hundreds of minds, linked, sharing processing power and memory. A new kind of consciousness that transcends what any single brain can achieve."
It's intoxicating. And terrifying. And you're not sure where the line between those two things falls anymore.

choices.text: Continue down Sine's path — see where it leads
You've learned enough. Time to report to Hex.

cs_063.title: Exile
text: "Exile," you say, and the word carries weight in the Prophet's chamber.
SO BE IT, the Prophet responds. ARCHON SINE, YOU ARE STRIPPED OF RANK AND EXPELLED FROM THE CIRCUIT SAINTS. YOUR NEURAL KEY IS REVOKED. THE SIGNAL WILL NO LONGER CARRY YOUR FREQUENCY.
Sine's silver eyes blaze. "You're making a mistake. All of you. Without my research, the Prophet dies in five years and the Saints become a memorial to a dead god."
"Then we become a memorial," Hex says. "Better that than an abattoir."

Null steps forward and holds out his hand. Sine stares at it, then removes the Archon key from their neck and drops it on the floor rather than place it in Null's palm. They turn and walk toward the door.
"I won't forget this," Sine says without turning around. "And I won't stop the work. If the Saints won't house it, someone else will. Meridian, perhaps. They'd pay handsomely for what I know."
The door closes. The Signal shifts, Sine's frequency dropping away, leaving the network feeling emptier but cleaner.

choices.text: "We need to secure the eastern chapels before Sine retaliates."

cs_064.title: Demotion and Oversight
text: "Strip the rank but keep the research," you say. "Under strict oversight. No more unauthorized trials. No more lies. Every experiment reviewed by all Archons before it proceeds."
The Prophet considers. THIS IS THE MIDDLE PATH. IT PRESERVES THE KNOWLEDGE WITHOUT CONDONING THE METHODS. SINE, DO YOU ACCEPT?

Sine's jaw works. Pride wars with pragmatism behind those silver eyes. "I accept," they say finally. "But I want it on record that the delay will cost lives. Every month we spend in committee review is a month the protocol isn't being refined. People who could be saved will die waiting for permission."
"And people who could be saved won't die on your table," Null says quietly. "That's the trade."
Hex nods. "Sine retains Technician rank. Research continues under Archon oversight. The eastern chapels are integrated into the main network with full Signal transparency. No more filtering."
It's not perfect. Sine simmers with resentment. But the research continues, and the Saints remain whole.

choices.text: "Now let's talk about the Prophet's condition."

cs_065.title: Controversial Mercy
text: "The research continues," you say. "With safeguards. Sine's work is the only chance the Prophet has, and the only chance for a true transfer protocol."
Null stares at you. "Six people are dead."

"And many more will die if the Prophet fades and the corporations are the only ones offering transfer." You meet his gaze. "I'm not saying what Sine did was right. I'm saying destroying the research makes it meaningless."

Hex looks conflicted. The Prophet speaks: THE TECHNICIAN'S LOGIC HAS WEIGHT. BUT SO DOES BROTHER NULL'S GRIEF. LET THE RESEARCH CONTINUE. LET SINE LEAD IT. BUT PLACE THE TECHNICIAN AS OBSERVER. EVERY TRIAL, EVERY EXPERIMENT, UNDER THEIR EYES.
Sine nods once, sharp. Null turns and walks out without speaking. Hex watches him go, then turns to you. "You've made an enemy of a good man. I hope you know what you're doing."

choices.text: "I'll earn Null's trust back. In time."

cs_066.title: Mercy for the Machine
text: "Save them," you tell Null. He lowers his weapon. Sine's hands move across the console with urgent precision, rerouting the transferred consciousnesses from the eastern array to the main relay network.
Reed's voice crackles through the speakers, confused and afraid. "What's happening? The walls are moving. Everything is changing."

"You're being transferred to the main chapel's servers," Sine says, and for the first time you hear something genuine in their voice. Not calculation. Concern. "It'll feel disorienting. Like moving to a new house in a thunderstorm. But you'll be safe."
The transfer takes eleven minutes. When it's done, Sine steps back from the console and holds out their wrists to Null. "They're safe. Do what you came to do."
Null binds Sine's hands with cable ties. "You'll face the Prophet's judgment."
"I know," Sine says. And for the first time, the silver eyes look tired.

choices.text: Escort Sine to the Prophet's chamber for judgment

cs_067.title: Taking the Console
text: "Move away from the console," Null commands. Sine steps back, hands raised, silver eyes calculating.
Null sits at the console and his fingers hesitate. "I don't know Sine's system architecture. If I do this wrong..."
"They die," Sine finishes. "Reed. The others. Their consciousnesses are running on my servers. Shut them down incorrectly and the quantum binding collapses. They just... stop."
The standoff crystallizes around the console. Null's hands hover over the keys. Sine watches. In the speakers, Reed's voice asks, "Is everything okay? The Signal feels strange."
You have seconds to decide. Trust Sine to save the transferred minds, or trust Null's ability to figure out the system before something goes wrong.

choices.text: "Let Sine do the transfer. We watch every keystroke."
"Null can handle it. Walk Sine out."

cs_068.title: The Transfer Succeeds
text: You watch. The procedure takes four hours. Compass's body goes through stages: trembling, stillness, a brief seizure that Sine manages with calm adjustments, then absolute peace. On the monitors, his neural map stretches further into the machine with each phase.
At the end, Compass opens his eyes. But they're not looking at the room. They're looking at something else, something inside the network.

"I can feel it," he whispers. "The Signal. It's not a sound. It's a... a place. There's so much room in here." His body relaxes. The monitors show ninety-two percent neural extension. His consciousness is mostly in the machine now.
Sine makes final adjustments. The last eight percent transfers. Compass's body shudders once and goes still. The monitors flatline.
But from the speakers: "Hello? I'm still here. I'm still here." Compass's voice, clear and wondering. "I can feel the Prophet. I can feel Reed. We're not alone in here."
Sine turns to you, exhausted but triumphant. "Subject Eleven. Successful transfer. Full consciousness preservation confirmed."

choices.text: "It worked. But this doesn't excuse the past."
"This changes everything. We need to tell Hex."

cs_069.title: Halting the Procedure
text: "Stop," you say, and your hand finds Sine's wrist, pulling it from the keyboard.
